title: An Astronaut’s Guide To Life on Earth

chapters.title: Becoming an astronaut is hard, and the job leaves no room for error
chapters.text:
Spaceflight stirs mixed feelings, especially for first timers. Chris Hadfield, the first Canadian to walk in space, said the experience packed equal doses of excitement and fear. Walking to the launchpad left him with a vague dread, and his mind ran through every way the launch could fail. Once he reached space, he felt oddly calm and got to work with a wide grin on his face.

An astronaut does more than travel into space. The job calls for fast information processing and fast decisions.

Astronauts often act on incomplete information, and the consequences of those choices can decide whether they and the rest of the crew live or die.

Astronauts are like public servants. They take on hard tasks for their country. The path takes years of nonstop, grueling training to prepare for a flight. Chris Hadfield has said that constant flights are one of the best ways for an astronaut to grow. By his third flight, he had already become one of the most senior officers in the program.

This summary walks through astronaut life, with attention to the strict exercises and training that shape it. You will learn more about spaceflight and what it takes to wear the suit.

chapters.title: A first mission is a door into vast experience and better choices
chapters.text:
For an astronaut, there is no such thing as too much information. You should stay open to learning no matter how skilled or seasoned you are, because another test always waits around the corner. The job demands years of intense preparation. You keep working through exams and drills across many subjects.

Changes in your life shape your ability to fly. One of the verification hurdles you must clear is a medical checkup and a mental assessment. A small health problem can disqualify you from spaceflight. Once you are airborne, there is almost no margin for error, and any health issue puts your clearance at risk.

A positive mindset helps you get ready for both good and bad days.

Space travel reads more as a perk than a right. It depends on a long list of variables no one controls, so putting your faith in luck is a poor plan. The right attitude matters for an astronaut’s mental strength and survival. In Hadfield’s case, he did good work because he did not stake his whole identity on his mission. He showed up, did the job, and tried to stay in the moment. Holding the right view of what being an astronaut means is the same as building the right attitude. The mission’s survival and the survival of everyone on board and on the ground ride on the team’s mindset. As an astronaut, you accept that you will pick up a set of unusual skills. You may never use some of them. Your attitude gets tested almost constantly.

Early success is a poor teacher. You get rewarded for not preparing, and when you finally face a moment that demands preparation, you do not know how. ~ Chris Hadfield

It also helps to spend as much time as possible studying flight rules. Another habit to get used to is debriefing. Debriefing happens on a regular schedule to look at what could have gone better, how it could have been done, and why it should have been done.

In 2017, only 12 candidates were selected by NASA out of 18,000 who applied.

chapters.title: Fear grows when you cannot predict what comes next
chapters.text:
Whatever your worries are, you have to learn to handle them. Find the source of your fear and try to shape your response to it. It does not matter if the fear stays. As long as you can steer it, you are in good shape.

Success is not guaranteed just because you feel ready. Being ready only prepares you for what could happen.

Astronauts prepare for bad news through simulations. They are pushed to face failure and then taught how to deal with it well. The point is to drill the response until it becomes automatic, so you know what to do in similar moments without thinking.

Training builds the instincts and reflexes that let you handle a bad moment without losing focus, balance, or concentration. You have to face your fears and learn to manage them before you can beat negative thinking.

chapters.title: An astronaut watches every detail to lower the chance of avoidable mistakes
chapters.text:
Some details look small or even pointless, but they matter when you pay attention to them. For astronauts, a beginner’s mindset is a smart way to learn. When Chris Hadfield realized he was failing his jet training exams, he chose to study in the aircraft rather than in his quarters. That move let him check items off his list and learn what kinds of questions he would face.

A good astronaut does not make the mistake of assuming he knows everything. Overconfidence almost always leads to failure.

There is no such thing as over preparation for an astronaut. The more you prepare, the better your odds. Astronauts are always under scrutiny. People study them at regular intervals, looking for flaws to fix. Since others will read your errors as lessons, learning to live with criticism and review is wise.

Early success is a poor teacher. You get rewarded for a lack of preparation, and when you finally face a moment that demands preparation, you do not know how. ~ Chris Hadfield

It also helps to spend as much time as possible studying flight rules. Another habit to get used to is debriefing. Debriefing happens on a regular schedule to look at what could have gone better, how it could have been done, and why it should have been done.

In 2017, only 12 candidates were selected by NASA out of 18,000 who applied.

chapters.title: Teamwork is the closest thing to a guarantee of survival and success
chapters.text:
No one becomes an astronaut by accident or luck. New astronauts are about 34 years old on average because of the long training path. The selection is also brutal. In 2009, only two astronauts were picked from a pool of 5,351 applicants in Canada. You have to be fiercely competitive when you apply. Early success does not prepare you for moments when things go sideways.

Astronauts must perform at every moment. You learn to work with people from many backgrounds. You have to read the people around you and remember that astronauts are competitive by nature. Your crewmates are the last people on the planet who can save you if things go wrong in space. Invest in other people’s success, because a healthy atmosphere leads to a healthy result.

Becoming an astronaut is not easy, and full focus is required at all times, especially when flying in formation. Formation flight makes it easy to keep an eye on each other, and it also makes it easy to take each other out if focus slips.

If you start thinking that only your biggest and shiniest moments count, you are setting yourself up to feel like a failure most of the time. ~ Chris Hadfield

Focus lets you read a situation quickly while staying balanced. Picture flying at 150 feet and a problem pops up. There is not much room to act. Focus does not promise safety, but it lifts your odds of survival when something goes wrong.

Being an astronaut means accepting that survival is not guaranteed the moment you board the shuttle.

Plenty of situations outside your control can threaten your life. You have to stay locked in on your surroundings at all times. A lapse can spell disaster for you or for the whole crew.

chapters.title: Knowing your role and when to switch gears shapes an astronaut’s success
chapters.text:
Astronauts keep building the mindset and skills they need to handle what comes at them. The simulations only prepare them for something close to the real thing, and the real event feels very different. The full mission demands thorough preparation. You cannot fake formulas or guess at decisions. You have to know what you are doing and do it well. Survival takes both physical and mental adjustment to a new setting.

It is easier to move through hard moments, big or small, when your mind is in the right state.

Many people, including astronauts, see the job as something close to being a superhero. Films have helped that image by showing astronauts as heroes who step in at the right moment. Crew members are seen in three ways. A minus one is someone who actively makes things harder and creates problems. A zero one is someone whose contributions neither help nor hurt the team in a meaningful way. A plus one is someone who keeps adding value for the team.

Everyone wants to be a plus one, but a zero is the safer target. The truth is that most candidates have no real sense of what the job takes. Their mental picture leaves out the long exams and reading lists required to clear a flight. The plus one role is for people ready to take risks and own the blame if anything slips. They can be intense and relentless. You can still earn respect as a zero one, as long as you do your part well.

If you enter a new setting aiming to burst out as a plus one, you risk causing chaos instead. Working in a group is easier as a zero one. To become a plus one, you have to read the room and your own strengths. Without that, even the most skilled and seasoned person turns into a minus one.

A genuine plus one earns quiet appreciation because they do not flash their talent in other people’s faces.

chapters.title: Adjusting to life off Earth is a steep learning curve for astronauts
chapters.text:
When Chris Hadfield and his crew left Earth to dock with the International Space Station, it was a once in a lifetime moment. The ISS is a million pound spacecraft about the size of a football field. Life on board was exciting and new for Hadfield. Even with the latest technology on board, parts of the ship made the experience strange. There was no flowing water because of microgravity. Privacy was almost nonexistent, and fresh food was in short supply.

As fun as orbit can be, hard moments demand full effort. You have to know how to switch between a zero one and a plus one when the situation calls for help. The life of an astronaut, especially a senior one, takes strong leadership.

Launching from Earth is as hard as returning to it. Different procedures, same feelings.

The feelings of leaving Earth and coming back are similar, even though the procedures are completely different. Landing, like launching, demands full attention and focus. Skipping precautions can create situations that put the whole crew at risk. When Hadfield and his crewmates were leaving space on the Soyuz, their helmets got stuck. Hadfield had to hold the situation together and steady everyone to keep a small problem from becoming a catastrophe.

Coming back to Earth is hard work. The pilots and astronauts had to wait at least an hour after the Soyuz landed for more than 150 control switches to be turned off before they could climb out. After that, everyone on board had to readjust to gravity and re learn an environment that might feel foreign after a long stretch in orbit.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
A true astronaut does not only dwell on the brightest moments. They try to enjoy every part of the journey. The mission is not over when you land on Earth. Months of debriefing, rehab, and medical tests still await from NASA and the ISS. A real astronaut does the work, values humility, and knows when to step back.

Life in space is fun, and it is not only serious work. The new setting and new experiences are something to savor.

Off Earth, you can focus on the pleasures and surprises, or you can frown at the frustrations. It is a choice. The job of an astronaut goes beyond doing tasks in space. It is also a way to share what is happening up there.

The best way to climb down a ladder is to appreciate and celebrate what you did while you were on top. The astronaut life is not as easy as it looks, and it is not as hard as it seems. Through the eyes of an expert, Chris Hadfield has explained what to expect and what not to expect on Earth and in space. It takes a lot to be an astronaut, but once you have lived in space, you realize it is worth every bit of the stress.

For anyone who wants to follow the path, unlearn what you think you know and accept that the ride is long. Believe in yourself and study every detail you can. That is how a great astronaut is built.
